_comment: LLARS Demo Video - IJCAI 2026 Demo Track
_version: 12.0 - GPT-5 Mini eval, pre-seeded evaluation scenario with progress states, IRR data
_design_principles: Live collab with a real second browser
Dialogue style: moderator explains, guest asks questions
_tts_naming: TTS models say 'Lars' (without the leading L) because they mispronounce 'LLARS'. In all written text, overlays, and UI, the correct name is 'LLARS'.

steps._section: === INTRO (50s) — Problem animation, pipeline overview with highlights, credentials, login ===
steps.speaker: moderator
steps.narration: Hey everyone, I'm Alex — and welcome to this demo of Lars, the LLM Assisted Research System. I'm here with my colleague David, and together we'll walk you through the platform. But first — the problem we're solving.

steps.speaker: guest
steps.narration: Thanks, Alex. The challenge is clear: domain experts know what quality means, but developers know how to build the pipelines. These two groups usually work in isolation — passing prompts back and forth in shared documents, with no version control and no systematic evaluation.

steps.speaker: moderator
steps.narration: Exactly — a lot of translation work between disciplines. That's what LLARS solves. Here's the pipeline.

steps.speaker: moderator
steps.narration: Here's the pipeline. Teams start in Prompt Engineering, where domain experts and developers co-author prompts together in real time. Those prompts feed into Batch Generation, which runs them across multiple models and datasets. And everything flows into Evaluation, where humans and LLMs assess the outputs side by side — with live agreement metrics.

steps.speaker: moderator
steps.narration: The credentials are on screen if you'd like to follow along live. Let me log us in.

steps.speaker: guest
steps.narration: I've read the paper and I'm curious to see this in action — let's dive in!

steps.speaker: moderator
steps.narration: We start with Prompt Engineering. This is where teams build and refine their prompts together.

steps.speaker: moderator
steps.narration: Let's create a new prompt. I can add collaborators right away — so I'll invite an IJCAI reviewer to work on this with us.

steps.speaker: guest
steps.narration: Oh interesting — so you're adding me as a collaborator. Does that mean we can both edit this prompt live at the same time?

steps.speaker: moderator
steps.narration: Exactly — every edit, every new block, you'll see it appear in real time. Let me create this and show you.

steps.speaker: moderator
steps.narration: Now, prompts in Lars are built from named blocks. I'll create three: Role Definition, Task Explanation, and Data Format.

steps.speaker: guest
steps.narration: I like that — instead of one big text box, domain experts see exactly where their instructions go. That makes it much easier to iterate on specific parts.

steps.speaker: moderator
steps.narration: Right. Let me fill these in now — a counselling assistant role, the extraction task, and the data format with placeholders.

actions.text: You are a counselling assistant who helps professionals extract key facts from client communications in online psychosocial counselling.
Read the email thread and write a brief situation overview in 2-3 sentences. Focus on the client's living situation, family, health, and main concerns. Write in third person, present tense.

steps.speaker: guest
steps.narration: Ah — those double curly braces, subject and content — those are placeholders?

steps.speaker: moderator
steps.narration: Yes — those are template variables. I can fill them with sample data right here to test the prompt. Let me add an email subject and a thread.

actions.text:
Client wrote on 05/01/2026 at 6:12 PM:
I am writing because my 14-year-old son has refused to go to school for the past three weeks. Every morning he complains of headaches and stomach aches, but our paediatrician found nothing physically wrong. Before this started, he was an average student and had a small group of friends. My son will not tell me what happened. His form teacher called and said he has 15 unexcused absences now. I am a single father and I have already used all my remaining leave days from work to stay home with him. I am worried about his grades and whether the school will involve the youth welfare office.

Counsellor wrote on 06/01/2026 at 10:00 AM:
Thank you for writing. School refusal at this age often signals that something significant has changed in the social environment. The physical symptoms your son reports are real — they are a stress response, even without an organic cause. It may help to gently explore whether there has been conflict with peers. Sometimes children at this age experience social exclusion or bullying but feel ashamed to talk about it. I would suggest requesting a meeting with the school counsellor, ideally without your son present at first, to understand what the school has observed. The youth welfare office is usually involved only in prolonged cases and their role is supportive, not punitive.

Client wrote on 08/01/2026 at 9:30 PM:
I spoke with his form teacher again today. She mentioned that a group of boys in his class has been making comments about his clothes and his phone being old. She was not sure if it counts as bullying. When I carefully asked my son about it tonight, he started crying and said everyone hates him and he never wants to go back. He also said he has been eating lunch alone for two months. I feel terrible that I did not notice earlier.

steps.speaker: guest
steps.narration: So you define sample values here to try different inputs and make sure the prompt works — without switching to another tool.

steps.speaker: moderator
steps.narration: Exactly — quick iteration. Let me hit Test — the variables get inserted and the LLM generates a response right here.

steps.speaker: guest
steps.narration: That's really convenient. You can see the rendered prompt with the actual data and immediately check the output quality.

steps.speaker: moderator
steps.narration: And if the output misses key facts or includes speculation, I just tweak the instructions and test again. No context switching, no re-uploading data.

steps.speaker: guest
steps.narration: Nice feedback loop. And can multiple people edit the same prompt at the same time?

steps.speaker: moderator
steps.narration: Yes — remember, I added you as a collaborator when we created the prompt. David, go ahead and log in on the right side — you should see it shared with you.

steps.speaker: guest
steps.narration: Alright, I'm logged in. And yes — I can see the Situation Summary prompt right here. Let me open it.

steps.speaker: moderator
steps.narration: There you go — your cursor appears on my screen. Every edit is tracked per user, so you always know who wrote what.

actions.text: The data below is provided as a subject line followed by the email thread content from a counselling session.

steps.speaker: guest
steps.narration: That's like Google Docs for prompts — no more translation work between disciplines. And the different colors — is that how you tell who wrote what?

steps.speaker: moderator
steps.narration: Exactly — each collaborator gets a unique color. You can customize it in your profile settings.

steps.speaker: guest
steps.narration: Ah, now I see it: when you type, Alex, version control tracks it.

steps.speaker: moderator
steps.narration: Exactly — every change is tracked per block and per user. You can commit snapshots, see diffs, and roll back. Let me open the full version history.

steps.speaker: moderator
steps.narration: Good. Now we have two prompts — the one we just built together, and a second one created during setup with slightly different instructions. We'll compare both in Batch Generation.

steps.speaker: moderator
steps.narration: Prompts are portable — you can download them as JSON or import blocks from other prompts.

steps.speaker: moderator
steps.narration: Lars ships with built-in Mistral models, but you can bring your own. Let me add OpenAI — we'll need it later for LLM-as-evaluator.

steps.speaker: guest
steps.narration: So each user can connect their own API keys? That's nice — no shared credentials.

steps.speaker: moderator
steps.narration: Exactly — credentials stay with the user who added them. Let me save and sync the available models.

steps.speaker: guest
steps.narration: And now you pick which models to enable? I see GPT-5 Nano and GPT-5 in the list.

steps.speaker: guest
steps.narration: So we have two prompts now. What's the next step — how do we actually run them against data?

steps.speaker: moderator
steps.narration: That's where Batch Generation comes in. We create a job that runs our prompts against a dataset with multiple models at once.

steps.speaker: moderator
steps.narration: First, the data. Remember those template variables — subject and content? This is where they come in. I'll paste ten counselling cases as JSON — each entry has a subject and content field that matches our variables. Lars fills them in automatically for every case.

steps.speaker: guest
steps.narration: And now we pick which prompts to use? I see both our prompts listed here.

steps.speaker: moderator
steps.narration: Exactly — both selected. Next we pick models. I'll take Mistral Small and GPT-5 Nano — one open-source, one commercial — so we can compare how they handle counselling case extraction.

steps.speaker: moderator
steps.narration: I'll name this job and we can review the full configuration.

steps.speaker: guest
steps.narration: Oh nice, a generation matrix. So that's ten items…

steps.speaker: moderator
steps.narration: …times two prompts, times two models — forty situation descriptions total. And notice the cost estimate — Lars calculates expected tokens and cost before you run anything.

steps.speaker: moderator
steps.narration: While that job runs in the background, let me open a completed batch so we can look at the actual outputs.

steps.speaker: guest
steps.narration: Can we look inside one of these outputs?

steps.speaker: moderator
steps.narration: Here's the full trace — rendered prompt on the left, model output on the right, plus metadata. The provenance is fully tracked — you always know which prompt and model produced each output.

steps.speaker: guest
steps.narration: OK, so we have forty situation descriptions — Mistral versus GPT-5 Nano, each with two different prompts. How do you decide which combination actually did better?

steps.speaker: moderator
steps.narration: From a completed batch, we can open the Scenario Wizard right away.

steps.speaker: moderator
steps.narration: Lars supports Rating with multi-dimensional Likert scales, Labeling, and pairwise Comparison — but for comparing outputs across models, Ranking is the best fit. Let's pick that.

steps.speaker: moderator
steps.narration: The name is prefilled from the batch. We move to configuration.

steps.speaker: moderator
steps.narration: The wizard previews the ranking buckets and lets you configure how items are distributed across evaluators.

steps.speaker: guest
steps.narration: So you can choose whether everyone evaluates everything, or split items across evaluators — and randomize the order to reduce bias?

steps.speaker: moderator
steps.narration: Exactly. We add ourselves as a human evaluator. And for LLM evaluation, I'll pick Magistral — our reasoning model — and GPT-5 Mini, so we get cross-provider comparison without breaking the budget.

steps.speaker: moderator
steps.narration: And we create the scenario. Now, we actually set one up earlier and both reviewers have been ranking — let me show you the progress.

steps.speaker: moderator
steps.narration: This is the Scenario Workspace. Both IJCAI reviewers have been evaluating, and Magistral and GPT-5 Mini ran as LLM judges.

steps.speaker: moderator
steps.narration: Overview shows live progress — you can see how many rankings each evaluator has completed.

steps.speaker: guest
steps.narration: So the evaluator list shows both reviewers and both LLM judges, along with how far each one has progressed.

steps.speaker: moderator
steps.narration: Each of the ten counselling cases has four situation descriptions — two prompts times two models. Reviewer one has finished eight, item nine is in progress, and item ten is still open. Reviewer two and both LLM evaluators are already done.

steps.speaker: moderator
steps.narration: The Data tab shows every item's evaluation status. Green means done, yellow means in progress, and grey means not started yet — the evaluator can pick up where they left off at any time.

steps.speaker: moderator
steps.narration: The Evaluation tab shows live agreement metrics — Krippendorff's alpha and pairwise agreement. Since both reviewers and both LLMs have been ranking, we already have data.

steps.speaker: guest
steps.narration: The heatmap shows pairwise agreement — reviewer one, reviewer two, Magistral, and GPT-5 Mini, all side by side. You can see exactly where humans and LLMs agree or disagree.

steps.speaker: moderator
steps.narration: Exactly — you can immediately see where humans and LLMs disagree, and whether it's systematic. These metrics update live as rankings come in.

steps.speaker: moderator
steps.narration: Exactly. And if it is, you go back to Prompt Engineering, refine the criteria, and re-evaluate. That's the iterative loop. You can also export everything as JSON or CSV.

steps.speaker: moderator
steps.narration: Finally, the Team tab shows who is involved and how many rankings each evaluator has completed.

steps.speaker: guest
steps.narration: Absolutely — you can add or remove humans and LLM evaluators at any point, even mid-evaluation.

steps.speaker: moderator
steps.narration: Now let's see the evaluator's perspective. As reviewer one, I have two rankings left to finish.

steps.speaker: guest
steps.narration: I can see which items are done in green, and which are still pending. Let me open one of the remaining ones.

steps.speaker: moderator
steps.narration: Notice the generic labels — Item A, B, C. The evaluator never sees model names. And the order is randomized for each evaluator to prevent position bias. You drag situation descriptions into buckets to rank them.

steps.speaker: guest
steps.narration: So it's fully blinded. No model names, no prompt labels, and a different order for everyone. That's solid methodology.

steps.speaker: moderator
steps.narration: Exactly — unbiased by design.

steps.speaker: moderator
steps.narration: We showed this with counselling data because that's our research domain — psychologists and social workers use Lars to craft prompts for extracting situation descriptions from client communications. In interviews, domain experts told us Lars eliminates the translation work between disciplines — they can directly co-author prompts and run evaluations without needing technical skills. But the platform is domain-agnostic — swap the email threads for medical reports or legal documents, and the workflow stays the same.

steps.speaker: guest
steps.narration: Bridging the gap between domain experts and developers — that's the core idea. Thanks, Alex.

steps.speaker: moderator
steps.narration: Thanks David. Lars is open-source — the credentials are on screen if you want to try it yourself. Check the repo and let us know what you think!
